rationale: Full independent re-review after commits 0d79943/f124494 replaced the disproven unique-nanosecond mtime scheme with a monotonic whole-second advance (_write captures the pre-write mtime; _advance_mtime stamps max(previous+1, int(time.time()))) and the test pins it deterministically (future-pinned mtime, strict whole-second increase across the mutant write and the restore write). All probes executed via rdm story mutation-probe on rdm/story_audit/mutation.py (the module under review IS the probe machinery: the running process keeps the imported original while the pytest subprocess re-imports the mutant). Clause table -- Core (test_mutation_probe_applies_reports_and_restores): (1) applies the mutation live: writing the original instead of original.replace(find,replace,1) KILLED (stub runner asserts 'VALUE = 2' on disk during the run); (2) reports killed: '"killed": outcome == TESTS_FAILED' -> '!=' KILLED; (3) reports survived: '"survived": outcome == TESTS_PASSED' -> '!=' KILLED; (4) always restores: finally-block restore write -> pass KILLED (file==original after killed AND survived paths, plus res['restored']); (5) exactly-once application (ambiguous site rejected): occurrences != 1 -> < 1 KILLED. Exit-code discrimination (test_mutation_probe_only_a_genuine_test_failure_is_a_kill): (6) only a genuine test failure is a kill: 'if proc.returncode == 1:' -> 'if proc.returncode:' (old any-nonzero behavior) KILLED (exit-5 must come back as error, not TESTS_FAILED); (7) an unclean run is an error, never a kill: dropping the 'outcome not in (TESTS_PASSED, TESTS_FAILED)' guard ('if False:') KILLED; (8) no-tests-collected identified: exit-5 condition '== 5' -> '== 55' KILLED. Defense in depth (test_mutation_probe_restore_survives_interruption): (9) journal-before-mutating: journaling '' instead of the original KILLED (runner reads the journal mid-window); journal removed after a normal probe: unlink -> exists KILLED; (10) interrupted-probe recovery: neutering the restore write in recover_interrupted_probe (_write -> bare read) KILLED (res['recovered'], file==original, journal gone, recover_interrupted_probe(src) False after) -- note the recovery path now goes through _write, so the prior verdict's file_path.write_text probe was retargeted; (11) SIGTERM converted to an exception: handler body raise -> pass KILLED (pytest.raises(KeyboardInterrupt) + restored + no journal); (12) whole-second bytecode-cache invalidation, the clause that made the prior verdict partial: 'fresh = max(previous + 1, int(time.time()))' -> 'max(previous, ...)' (loses the strict advance) KILLED, and '_advance_mtime(file_path, previous)' -> '_advance_mtime(file_path, int(file_path.stat().st_mtime))' (post-write clock capture, the old broken ordering) KILLED -- both die on the future-pinned 'future < mtime_seconds[0] < after' assertion, which is exactly the (mtime-seconds, size) pyc-key property. Empirical confirmation: the size-preserving survived-inversion mutant ('"survived": outcome == TESTS_PASSED' -> '== TESTS_FAILED', both tokens 12 chars) that previously SURVIVED 2 of 3 runs on stale bytecode now KILLED 3/3 consecutive runs; it is recorded as a replayable killing probe. The prior verdict's handler-NOT-installed mutant (signal.signal -> signal.getsignal) is NOT re-recorded: it replays as ERROR (the re-raised TERM kills the inner pytest itself, exit -15) -- not evidence either way; the clause is pinned by the neutered-handler kill instead. Totals this review: 17 probe executions, 17 KILLED, 0 SURVIVED, 0 ERROR. No uncovered clauses remain.

probes.find: what = ("no tests matched the selector" if proc.returncode == 5
probes.replace: what = ("no tests matched the selector" if proc.returncode == 55
pass  # SIGTERM ignored by the handler
